reasoning:
The finding claims SSRF via path traversal, but the host is not attacker-controlled. get_proxy_base_url() returns Filters::apply_sites_proxy_cdn_filters(), which hardcodes the destination to 'https://$portal_id.sites-proxy.hscoscdn<swimlane>.net' — a HubSpot CDN domain derived from the plugin's own stored portal_id. An attacker cannot redirect the outbound request to a different host.

The predefined path patterns (PREDEFINED_PATH_PATTERNS, lines 16-23) are anchored regexes that require the URI to start with /_hcms/, /hs/, /hubfs/, /hs-fs/, /cs/c/, or /e3t/. A request like /_hcms/../../etc/passwd does satisfy ~^/_hcms/.*$~ because .* matches any suffix. The raw $request_uri (after esc_url_raw()) would then be appended to the fixed HubSpot CDN base, yielding something like https://12345.sites-proxy.hscoscdn.net/../../etc/passwd.

However: (1) The destination host is entirely fixed to a HubSpot CDN — not internal infrastructure, not arbitrary URLs; (2) Both WordPress's HTTP client (WP_Http / cURL) and the HubSpot CDN will normalize path traversal sequences, so no unintended server-side path is actually reached; (3) The proxy feature is gated behind Portal_Options::get_proxy_mappings_enabled() — disabled by default unless the site owner has configured a HubSpot portal; (4) Even if a CDN path was traversed, the impact is reading a public CDN resource on HubSpot's servers, not accessing internal WordPress/server resources or arbitrary third-party hosts.

This is not a real SSRF. It is at most a cosmetic URL-normalization gap with zero practical exploit impact. Marking is_real=false.
